mnemonics.title: Constraint Order Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: Remember the sequence: 'P Keys Never Use Default Checks' - PRIMARY KEY, UNIQUE, NOT NULL, DEFAULT, CHECK. This helps when writing CREATE TABLE statements in the correct order.

mnemonics.title: The Key Family
mnemonics.content: PRIMARY KEY is the 'Dad' - one per table, makes final decisions. FOREIGN KEY is the 'Kid' - references another table's dad. UNIQUE is the 'Aunt' - must be different from everyone but not the main boss.

mnemonics.title: NULL Remembering Tip
mnemonics.content: Remember: NULL means 'nothing' or 'unknown' - it's NOT zero, NOT an empty string. Think of NULL as an empty box vs. a box with nothing in it.

acronyms.usage: Uniquely identifies each row in a table. Only ONE per table. Cannot contain NULL values.
Creates a relationship between two tables. References PK in another table. Can have multiple FKs per table.
Ensures a column cannot have NULL values. Makes a field mandatory/required.
Ensures all values in a column are different. Allows one NULL (in most DBs). Multiple UQ allowed per table.
Limits the range of values that can be placed in a column. User-defined condition.
Sets a default value for a column when no value is specified during insertion.

visualTips.visualization: Draw a star ★ at the top of the primary key column. The star represents 'the one and only' - unique and essential. Only ONE star per table.
Draw arrows pointing FROM your table TO the referenced table. Like a child holding hands with their parent - connected but pointing outward.
Draw a solid black circle ● on the column header. This represents a 'filled' box that must always contain something - never empty.
Draw an empty circle ○ with a slash through it. Like a 'no duplicates allowed' stamp. The column can be empty (NULL) but if filled, must be unique.
Draw a small fence 🪜 around acceptable values. Like a bouncer at a club - only lets in values that meet the condition.
Draw tables as boxes with FK columns highlighted in a different color. Connect FK to the PK it references with a line ending in a dot (crow's foot) showing 'many' side.

analogies.analogy: Like a Social Security Number - it's your unique identity. Everyone has one (or should), and no two people share the same number. Just like a table needs one PK to identify each row uniquely.
Like a home address. Your address doesn't identify YOU (that's the PK), but it tells others WHERE you live (references another table). It connects you to a location (parent table).
Like a required field on a job application. Under 'Email' or 'Phone', there's no empty line allowed - you MUST fill it in. That field can never be blank.
Like a license plate number. Two cars can have the same color, make, or model, but NEVER the same license plate. Every value must be different, but NULL is like a car not yet registered.
Like age restrictions at a movie theater. The rule says 'You must be 18 or older to watch this R-rated film.' The CHECK constraint works the same way - it enforces a condition or rule on values.
Like the default reply 'Thank you' on an automated email when you don't write a personalized message. If you don't provide a value, the system automatically fills in what's pre-set.
